coredata.dc:title: Confining carbon dots in extra-large pore zeolite SYSU-3 assembled in a mixed-template system
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Jiali
Zhang, Chuanqi
Zhang, Yang
Zeng, Zhifeng
Ma, Fei
Jiang, Jiuxing
coredata.dc:description: The 24-membered ring -SYT type extra-large pore zeolite, denoted as DESO-SYSU-3, has been synthesized by employing N,N′-diethylsophoridine and trimethylsulfanium in a mixed-template system, where the N,N′-diethylsophoridine and trimethylsulfanium played different roles in the crystallization process. A single usage of N,N′-diethylsophoridine led to the formation of an unknown phase (donated as SYSU-5). These results reveal that the utilization of alkaloid-derived organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs) is an efficient synthetic strategy to produce specific extra-large pore zeolites and related structures. Compared to the prototype SYSU-3, the addition of TMSF into the system not only improved the silicon content in the framework of DESO-SYSU-3 but also facilitated the generation of carbon dots (CDs) confined in extra-large pore structure. The structure and the proposed formation mechanism of CDs were investigated based on HRTEM, XPS, FI-IR, UV–Vis, Raman and photoluminescence spectrum. The CDs@zeolite composite exhibited intriguing optical properties of both fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, respectively. This work extends extra-large pore zeolite as the matrix for the development of CDs@zeolite composites with tunable optical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mixed-template method
Extra-large pore zeolite
Carbon dots
Room-temperature phosphorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of fluorine content by ion-selective electrode analysis
Preparation of the germanosilicate zeolite DESO-SYSU-3
Preparation of an unknown germanosilicate SYSU-5
Synthesis and characterization of DESO-SYSU-3 by the mixed-template method
The formation of CDs and photoluminescence of DESO-sysu-3
Synthesis and characterization of the unknown germanosilicate SYSU-5
The function of TMSF during the crystallization of DESO-SYSU-3

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21805316 and 22372200).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Powder X-ray Diffraction of as-made DESO-SYSU-3, comparing with the simulated diffraction of -SYT zeolite structure. (b) Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of rod-like DESO-SYSU-3. (c) Powder X-ray Diffraction of the unknown germanosilicate SYSU-5. (d) SEM image of plate-like SYSU-5.

(a) TEM image of DESO-SYSU-3 (inset, HRTEM image of the lattice spacing of the CDs) (b) Particle distribution of CDs in DESO-SYSU-3. (c) The steady-state photoluminescence spectrum and delayed photoluminescence spectrum of DESO-SYSU-3 under the excitation of 365 nm at room temperature. (d) Time-resolved decay spectra of DESO-SYSU-3 under the excitation of 360 nm. (e) The steady-state photoluminescence spectrum and delayed photoluminescence spectrum of DESO-SYSU-3 under the excitation of 365 nm at 77 K. (f) Proposed photoluminescence mechanism of DESO-SYSU-3.

(a) In-situ powder X-ray diffraction of as-made SYSU-5 from room temperature to 600 °C. (b) Thermogravimetric analysis result of as-made SYSU-5. (c) The 13C MAS NMR of as-made SYSU-5 and the liquid 13C NMR of DESO. (d) The 29Si MAS NMR of SYSU-5 with peak fitting result, the original data is shown in black line and the simulated result is shown in deep blue line.
(a) Comparison of the TMA and TMSF cations with corresponding sod and clo cages. (b) Proposed function for TMSF during the synthesis of DESO-SYSU-3.

head.ce:title: Confining carbon dots in extra-large pore zeolite SYSU-3 assembled in a mixed-template system

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Bioinorganic and Synthetic Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The 24-membered ring -SYT type extra-large pore zeolite, denoted as DESO-SYSU-3, has been synthesized by employing N,N′-diethylsophoridine and trimethylsulfanium in a mixed-template system, where the N,N′-diethylsophoridine and trimethylsulfanium played different roles in the crystallization process. A single usage of N,N′-diethylsophoridine led to the formation of an unknown phase (donated as SYSU-5). These results reveal that the utilization of alkaloid-derived organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs) is an efficient synthetic strategy to produce specific extra-large pore zeolites and related structures. Compared to the prototype SYSU-3, the addition of TMSF into the system not only improved the silicon content in the framework of DESO-SYSU-3 but also facilitated the generation of carbon dots (CDs) confined in extra-large pore structure. The structure and the proposed formation mechanism of CDs were investigated based on HRTEM, XPS, FI-IR, UV–Vis, Raman and photoluminescence spectrum. The CDs@zeolite composite exhibited intriguing optical properties of both fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, respectively. This work extends extra-large pore zeolite as the matrix for the development of CDs@zeolite composites with tunable optical properties.

In this work, N, N′-diethylsophoridine and trimethylsulfonium were combined to form a mixed-template system in the synthesis of germanosilicate zeolite. The mixed-template system not only produced the extra-large pore zeolite DESO-SYSU-3 with increased framework silicon content, but also facilitated the generation of carbon dots (CDs), which were confined in the zeolitic matrix and formed the composite with fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence. When N, N′-diethylsophoridine was used as the sole template, an unknown germanosilicate SYSU-5 was produced. The different roles of N, N′-diethylsophoridine and trimethylsulfonium in the synthesis process as well as the photoluminescence mechanism of CDs@composite were investigated.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The extra-large pore zeolites (defined by > 12 TO4 tetrahedra) have attracted great attention due to the industrial demands of adsorption, separation and catalysis of bulky molecules. Especially, the category of extra-large pore zeolites with pore openings larger than 20-membered rings (MRs) is still rare but highly demanded [1–3]. The organic structure-directing agents (OSDAs) derived from natural alkaloids possess unique molecular skeletons, which led to the production of several specific extra-large pore zeolites. For instance, the utilization of sophoridine derivative gave rise to the new zeolite structure SYSU-3 (-SYT, 24-MR) [4], and matrine derivative was shown to be effective in the synthesis of ITQ-43 (-IRT, 28-MR) and ITQ-37(-ITV, 30-MR) [5]. An enantio-enriched extra-large pore chiral zeolite GTM-3 with -ITV structure was recently prepared by using enantiopure OSDAs derived from N-methylephedrine [6]. Apart from the stable aluminosilicate EMM-23 (-EMT, 21-MR) [7], most of the zeolites with extra-large pores larger than 20 MRs are germanosilicates with interrupted structure (-SYT, -IRT, -ITV), which suffered from poor hydrothermal stability due to the susceptibility of the Ge–O bonds. Recently, a stable -IRT type zeolite was obtained by a combination of acid-wash decomposition and recrystallization using a simple commercial OSDA [8]. Moreover, many approaches have been developed in recent years to stabilize the Ge-rich zeolites, involving the substitution of stable atoms (Si, Al, Ti, B, etc.) for the Ge atom [9–12]. However, these protocols are not applicable to all extra-large pore germanosilicates, and it's particularly important to improve the stability of germanosilicate frameworks by a comprehensive use of different approaches.

The mixed-template approach combining OSDAs with pore-filling agents (PFAs) in zeolite synthesis was used to produce several zeolites, such as SSZ-25, ZSM-5 and SSZ-47 [13,14]. Many simple amines including isobutylamine, piperidine and cyclopentylamine have served as the PFAs during the zeolite crystallization. In these systems, the fast crystallization of the desired zeolites was produced by using very small amounts of OSDAs and larger amounts of PFAs, thus reducing the cost of zeolite synthesis. Notably, Ryoo et al. first introduced bulky organic sulfonium cations into the synthesis of extra-large pore germanosilicates with ISV-, ITT-type structures and “GeZA” with intersecting channels of 15 × 12 × 12 MR pores [15], and MEL-type aluminosilicate was obtained by using tributylsulfonium as the OSDA. It was found that trimethyl-, triethyl-, or tripropylsulfonium did not produce any crystalline silicate zeolite, indicating their structure-directing effects are not apparent, but they can serve as simple PFAs for the cost-efficiency synthesis of zeolites [16]. Therefore, we supposed that the combination of alkaloid-derived OSDAs with simple sulfonium cations may enhance the stability and reduce the cost of preparing extra-large pore zeolites.

On the other hand, as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have shown promising potential in optoelectronics [17], biological imaging [18–20], catalysis [21], and ion detection [22–24]. The “bottom-up” strategy using small organic molecules as precursors was widely adopted for preparing CDs materials [25,26]. Coincidently, the preparation of CDs under the hydrothermal condition of zeolite synthesis has the advantages of tuneable size and functionality, excellent dispersibility and stability. Initially, Yu et al. reported a “dots-in-zeolites” strategy of in situ confining CDs into zeolitic matrices [27], which endowed the materials with fascinating thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) and room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) afterglow properties. Since then, a series of long afterglow CDs@zeolite composites were obtained via hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis or post-treatment of zeolites [28–32]. In these cases, most of the zeolite matrices are aluminophosphate with small- or large-pore structures. Confining CDs in extra-large pore zeolites is worth to be explored to modulate the properties of CDs@zeolite composites.

In this work, we present a cost-efficient synthesis of 24-MR -SYT-type extra-large pore germanosilicate (denoted as DESO-SYSU-3) by using a small amount of alkaloid-derived OSDA N,N′-diethylsophoridine (DESO) and simple trimethylsulfonium hydroxide (TMSF) in the mixed-template approach. Compared to the prototype zeolite, the employment of TMSF as PFAs not only improved the Si content of DESO-SYSU-3 but also facilitated the formation of luminescent CDs to confine into the DESO-SYSU-3 matrix. The CDs@zeolite composite exhibited fluorescence and RTP emission, respectively. The roles of DESO and TMSF in the crystallization process as well as the luminescence mechanism of the composite were investigated based on a battery of characterizations. In addition, the employment of DESO as the sole OSDA produced an unknown germanosilicate denoted as SYSU-5.

Tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS, 99 %, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd), germanium dioxide (99.999 %, Acros Organics), hydrofluoric acid (HF, 49 wt% in water, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd), sophoridine (98 %, Xi'an Realin Biotechnology Co. Ltd), tetrahydrofuran (THF, 99 %, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd), lithium tetrahydroaluminate (LiAlH4, 2.5 mol/L in THF, Energy Chemical), trimethylsulfonium iodide (TMSF, 98 %, Energy Chemical). All reagents were used directly without further purification.

The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data were collected at Rigaku SmartLab diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) in the 2θ range of 4–40°. The step size was 0.01 and the scanning speed was 1°/min. Temperature-dependent PXRD patterns of SYSU-5 from room temperature to 600 °C were performed at Rigaku SmartLab diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image was taken on an Ultra-high Resolution FE-SEM SU8010 electron microscope. Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was performed on a TG209F1 libra thermogravimetry in air with a heating rate of 10 °C/min from room temperature to 800 °C. Inductively coupled plasma (ICP) analysis was performed on a PerkinElmer Optima 8300 spectrometer. Elemental analysis (EA) was conducted on an ELEMENTAR Vario EL Cube elemental analyzer. The liquid state 13C NMR was collected on a Bruker Advance III spectrometer with a nuclear magnetic resonance of 400 MHz. The solid-state 19F, 29Si and 13C MAS NMR spectra were collected on a VARIAN VNMRS-400WB spectrometer with the resonance frequencies of 400 MHz. The infrared absorption spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer Frontier spectrometer in the range of 450–4000 cm−1. Raman spectrum was obtained on a Renishaw Invia Raman spectrometer equipped with a 532 nm laser device. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer and BaSO4 was used for the standard reference in the measurements. Steady-state and transient photoluminescence and time-resolved spectrum were recorded on an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer in the air by using an Xe lamp and microsecond flash lamp (μF900) as the excitation source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of fluorine content by ion-selective electrode analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the fluorine content in DESO-SYSU-3 and SYSU-5 was measured by ion-selective electrode analysis. The sample dissolution and measurement steps were as follows: 20 mg of sample was mixed with 0.2 g NaOH in a Nickel crucible and heated at 550 °C for 20 min in a muffle furnace. Then the digested sample was dissolved in an appropriate amount of water and transferred into a 50 mL volumetric flask, followed by the addition of 0.2 mL hydrochloric acid (36.5 wt%). Deionized water was then used to dilute the solution to the tick mark. Before testing, 10.0 mL of the aforementioned solution was transferred into a 50 mL volumetric flask, with 1–2 drops of bromocresol purple added as an indicator. The pH of the tested solution was adjusted with hydrochloric acid drops until the color changed from violet to yellow. After that, 15 mL TISAB was added and the above solution was diluted to a final volume of 50 mL with deionized water. The test solution was transferred into polyethylene beakers and stirred continuously when the test was conducted. Fluoride ion-selective electrode and Ag–AgCl reference electrode were employed to determine the electrode potential. The standard curve was established using a series of fluoride ion solutions with known concentrations. The sample fluorine content was calculated based on the electrode potential of the test solution and the standard curve.

In a typical synthesis for DESO, 6.2 g (0.025 mol) (−)-sophoridine was mixed with 1.9 g (0.05 mol) lithium tetrahydroaluminate (LiAlH4) in 100 mL anhydrous tetrahydrofuran in a 250 mL round-bottomed flask and refluxed overnight. After cooling down to room temperature, the excess LiAlH4 was decomposed with distilled water. The mixture was filtered to remove the insoluble substance (Al(OH)3), getting the intermediate product Ⅰ with a yield of about 90 % (Fig. 1 ). The product Ⅰ was subsequently dissolved in 60 mL ethyl acetate, mixed with 7.8 g ethyl iodide (CH3CH2I, 0.05 mol) and refluxed for 12 h. The solid product was obtained by filtration and washed with diethyl ether three times. Before being used in the synthesis, the final product and TMSF were converted to hydroxide form using anionic exchange resin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the germanosilicate zeolite DESO-SYSU-3
ce:section.ce:para: A typical synthesis gel was prepared by dissolving 35 mg of germanium oxide in 2.3 g of DESO solution (0.09 mmol/g) and 1.6 g of TMSF solution (0.49 mmol/g). Then 347 mg of tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) was added to the solution and the mixture was stirred at room temperature until the TEOS was hydrolyzed completely. After the addition of 33 mg of HF, the appropriate amount of water was allowed to evaporate under a stream of air. The final gel with a molar composition of Si: Ge: DESO: HF: TMSF: H2O = 0.83: 0.17: 0.1: 0.4: 0.4: 3.0 was heated at 170 °C for two days. The solid product was filtered, washed with deionized water three times, and dried at 60 °C overnight to obtain a pure DESO-SYSU-3 sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of an unknown germanosilicate SYSU-5
ce:section.ce:para: A typical synthesis process for the unknown germanosilicate SYSU-5 was as follows: 70 mg of germanium oxide (0.67 mmol) was dissolved in 8.0 g of DESO solution (0.09 mmol/g). Then 278 mg of TEOS was added into the solution and stirred at room temperature until the TEOS was totally hydrolyzed. When the hydrolysis process was completed, 370 mg of 10%wt NH4F solution was added to the gel and formed a homogeneous gel. The final gel of SYSU-5 had a composition of Si: Ge: DESO: NH4F: H2O = 0.67: 0.33: 0.25: 0.5: 3.0. After crystallized at 170 °C for 2 days, the product was filtered, washed with deionized water for three times and dried at 60 °C overnight to obtain pure SYSU-5 sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of DESO-SYSU-3 by the mixed-template method
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the successful introduction of ethyl groups in DESO, NMR tests were performed on DESO. The 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of DESO are shown in Fig. S1. The signals at 1.20 ppm in 1H NMR and at 6.3 ppm in 13C NMR could be attributed to the ethyl groups in DESO, demonstrating the successful introduction of ethyl groups in the sophoridine skeleton.

The PXRD pattern of as-made DESO-SYSU-3 matched well with the simulated pattern of -SYT zeolite structure, indicating that high crystallinity DESO-SYSU-3 was obtained under the mixed-template system (Fig. 2 a). The SEM image displayed a rod-like morphology of the as-made DESO-SYSU-3, and no impurity was observed (Fig. 2b). ICP analysis of the DESO-SYSU-3 gave a framework Si/Ge ratio of 1.6 (Table S1), which is higher than the SYSU-3 reported in our previous work (about 1.1) [4]. Compared with the original synthetic gel composition listed in Table S2, the increase of Si content in the framework accompanied by the decrease in contents of DESO and GeO2 demonstrated that the mixed-template method is cost-efficient and effective in enhancing the framework Si/Ge ratio. The EA analysis result of DESO-SYSU-3 was listed in Table S3, giving a calculated carbon to nitrogen (C/N) ratio of 8.9, which is close to the C/N ratio of DESO (9.5). The amount of sulfur content in DESO-SYSU-3 was 0.22 wt%, giving a calculated DESO to TMSF molecule ratio of 11. The low content of sulfur in DESO-SYSU-3 was probably due to the heat-sensitive nature of TMSF, and a thermal decomposition of TMSF may occur during the crystallization process. Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis of DESO-SYSU-3 showed a total weight loss of about 30.3 % from room temperature to 800 °C, which could be divided into two stages of weight loss (Fig. S2). The first stage of 4.6 % up to 200 °C could be assigned to the removal of absorbed water, and the second one of 25.7 % in the range of 200–800 °C corresponded to the decomposition of organic species in the sample. The fluorine content of DESO-SYSU-3 was measured by the fluoride ion selective electrode method, giving a result of 0.65 wt% of fluorine in the sample. Combining the results above, the formula of DESO-SYSU-3 obtained in the mixed-template system was determined as |C19H36N2|7.5|C3H9S|0.69[Si49Ge31O160F3.4](H2O)x.

Solid-state NMR tests were performed on DESO-SYSU-3 to further explore the bonding situation in the material. The 29Si MAS NMR spectrum of DESO-SYSU-3 (Fig. S3a) could be divided into a prominent signal at −105.4 ppm (Si(OSi)4 species, denoted as Q4) and a weak component at −94 ppm (Si(OSi)3(OH) species denoted as Q3) [33,34], giving a Q3/Q4 ratio of 0.20 in DESO-SYSU-3, indicating the presence of silanol groups or defects in the structure. Comparison of 13C MAS NMR of DESO-SYSU-3 with those of DESO and TMSF revealed that the major part of DESO remained intact within the framework, while the signal of TMSF cation in DESO-SYSU-3 was not obvious, further indicating the low content of TMSF in DESO-SYSU-3 (Fig. S3b). The 13C MAS NMR band at around 80 ppm for DESO-SYSU-3 could be attributed to the decomposition product, which probably came from Hoffmann's decomposition of the ODSA during the hydrothermal synthesis process. The solid-state 19F NMR spectrum of DESO-SYSU-3 (Fig. S3c) exhibited a strong resonance band at δ = −8.9 ppm, corresponding to the F− ions located in the double four-ring (d4r) units with 3 or 4 germanium atoms [35]. The weak band at δ = −123.1 ppm was owing to the free F− ions located in the channels. The additional peak at δ = −128.4 ppm corresponded to the SiF6 2− species on the surface of the sample [36], which could be washed off by deionized water.

Combining the above results, the pure DESO-SYSU-3 with higher silicon content was obtained through the mixed-template method, which not only reduced the amount of OSDA and GeO2 in the synthetic gel but also enhanced the Si/Ge ratio in the zeolite. EA and 13C MAS NMR results of DESO-SYSU-3 revealed the low content of TMSF cation in the final product. The function of TMSF during the synthesis was explored in the following section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The formation of CDs and photoluminescence of DESO-sysu-3
ce:section.ce:para: The mixed-template method in this work not only affected the framework composition of DESO-SYSU-3 but also promoted the generation of CDs and the subsequent formation of CDs@zeolite composite, endowing DESO-SYSU-3 with optical properties of both fluorescence and RTP. The CDs particles could be observed in the TEM image with an average particle size of 3.3 nm (Fig. 3 a–b), which was larger than the pore opening of DESO-SYSU-3, indicating that the CDs particles may be confined in the interrupted structure of the crystals. As shown in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of Fig. 3a, the crystalline structure of CDs particles with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm could be observed, which was attributed to the (100) facet of graphene [37], demonstrating that the CDs formed under hydrothermal condition produced graphene-like carbon core. UV–vis spectrum of DESO-SYSU-3 exhibited an intense absorbance band at 275 nm with an extended shoulder peak at around 360 nm (Fig. S4), corresponding to the π-π* transitions of CC bonds and n-π* transitions from CN/CO bonds [38], respectively. Noting that the organic substances added in the synthetic gel do not contain conjugate groups, the conjugate structure should be generated from the condensation reaction of the organic species during the hydrothermal process, which formed CDs with luminous centers and becomes the origin for the photoluminescence.

A series of characterizations were conducted to reveal the structure of CDs. UV–vis spectrum of CDs solution (Fig. S5) displayed an intense signal at 260 nm, which was aroused by π-π* transitions from the conjugated domains. The subdominant peak at 295 nm was induced by n-π* transitions of the CN/CO functional groups [39,40]. The FTIR spectrum of the CDs exhibited absorbance bands at 3381, 2929, 2853, 1645 and 1565 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of –OH/-NH, –CH and –CC/-CN groups (Fig. S6) [41]. The signals of conjugate groups further demonstrated the decomposition and condensation of organic contents. The XPS survey of CDs also indicated the formation of the conjugated and functional groups [42,43], as shown in Fig. S7. The C 1s XPS spectrum could be fitted into three signals at 284.5, 285.5 and 288.5 eV, corresponding to CC, C–C/C–N and CO bands, respectively. The N 1s XPS survey could be divided into 398.8, 400.1 and 401.7 eV assigned to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively. The O 1s XPS spectrum could be fitted into two signals, the signals at 531.4 eV could be assigned to the CO band, while the signals at 532.1 eV could be attributed to the C–O bands. The various functional groups indicated the diverse surface states of CDs, resulting in various luminous centers. Raman spectrum of CDs exhibited two signals at 1360 (D band) and 1575 cm−1 (G band), respectively (Fig. S8). The former band corresponded to the amorphous carbon and the latter was assigned to the graphitic-like carbon in the materials, suggesting the typical core-shell structure of CDs [44,45].

Under the 365 nm excitation, DESO-SYSU-3 emitted cyan fluorescence at 470 nm, the corresponding photoluminescence spectrum was displayed in Fig. 3c. After the removal of the excitation source, the sample preserved a yellow-white afterglow at 575 nm, which could be observed by the naked eye for about 1 s (Fig. S9). Besides, the excitation spectrum exhibited a wide band ranging from 365 nm to 400 nm, with the optimal excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. S10). The excitation band of DESO-SYSU-3 overlapped with the n-π* transitions band in the UV–vis spectrum, indicating that the photoluminescence emission originated from the n-π* transitions of CDs. The corresponding chromaticity coordinates of the fluorescence and RTP for DESO-SYSU-3 were (0.24, 0.31) and (0.36, 0.40) (Fig. S11), respectively. The photoluminescence lifetime of DESO-SYSU-3 was measured, giving a result of 15.9 ns for the fluorescence and 17.4 ms for the RTP (Fig. 3d).

To further study the luminescence mechanism of DESO-SYSU-3, temperature-dependent photoluminescence of DESO-SYSU-3 was measured and analysed. With the increase of temperature from 77 K to 300 K, the location of fluorescence and afterglow emission peak remained unchanged, while the emission intensity gradually decreased, showing the typical photoluminescence behavior of RTP materials (Fig. S12). Moreover, the afterglow emission at 470 nm showed an increase in intensity with the increasing temperature (Fig. S12b), indicating that a portion of the emission was TADF. Under 365 nm excitation at 77 K, the DESO-SYSU-3 emitted fluorescence and phosphorescence at 470 nm and 575 nm (Fig. 3e), respectively. The calculated energy gap between the singlet state to triplet state (ΔE ST) was 0.44 eV, which was small enough for the occurrence of inter-system crossing (ISC) and reverse inter-system crossing (RISC) process, thus facilitating the generation of RTP and TADF emission.

To sum up, the employment of the mixed-template method could induce the formation of extra-large pore zeolite -SYT and promote the generation of CDs along with the decomposition of organic species in the gel. During the hydrothermal process, the CDs were subsequently in situ confined by the zeolitic matrix to form CDs@zeolite composite, endowing the material with photoluminescence properties. The proposed photoluminescence mechanism of DESO-SYSU-3 is displayed in Fig. 3f.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the unknown germanosilicate SYSU-5
ce:section.ce:para: The structure-directing ability of DESO has been investigated in a series of synthetic experiments, but no crystalline product was obtained in silicate/aluminosilicate synthetic systems. When the germanium was introduced into the synthetic reaction, an unknown germanosilicate material denoted as SYSU-5, was obtained. SYSU-5 exhibited characteristic PXRD diffraction at 2θ = 5.7° and 8.9° (Fig. 2c). As pictured in Fig. 2d, the SYSU-5 exhibited a plate-like morphology with a diameter of 3–4 μm and thickness of around 50–100 nm. Temperature-dependent XRD of SYSU-5 was measured from room temperature to 600 °C (Fig. 4 a), indicating that the structure could remain stable up to 300 °C. However, as the temperature continued to rise, the diffraction peaks of the sample were progressively weakened and shifted to a higher angle, suggesting the condensation tendency and the progressive decrease of crystallinity of the structure, demonstrating that SYSU-5 may be an open-framework germanosilicate. When the temperature reached 600 °C, the diffraction peaks almost disappeared, indicating that the crystal structure of SYSU-5 almost collapsed probably due to the structural mismatch of the stacking sheets.

The ICP and EA analysis results of SYSU-5 were listed in Table S1 and Table S3, giving a framework Si/Ge molar ratio of 1.58 and a C/N ratio result of 9.0, respectively. TG analysis of SYSU-5 showed a 2.0 wt% weight loss before 300 °C and a total weight loss of 24.2 wt% in the region of 300–800 °C (Fig. 4b). The former stage corresponded to the removal of adsorbed water and the latter stage was attributed to the decomposition of the organic species in SYSU-5 as well as the condensation of silica hydroxyl groups distributed between the sheets. A comparison between the solid-state 13C MAS NMR of SYSU-5 and the 13C NMR of the DESO is displayed in Fig. 4c, indicating that the major part of the DESO molecule was maintained intact in the framework of SYSU-5. The fluorine content of SYSU-5 was measured by the fluoride ion selective electrode method, showing that 0.44 wt% of the sample consisted of fluoride. Based on the characterization results above, the chemical formula of SYSU-5 was given as |C19H36N2|8.4[Si64Ge41O210F2.3](H2O)x.

A series of NMR tests were carried out to better understand the binding conditions in SYSU-5. The 29Si MAS-NMR of SYSU-5 could be fitted as 5 species (Fig. 4d), and the signals at −94.6 and −103.7 ppm corresponded to Q3 species in the framework. The proportion of the two peaks was 18.3 % and 25.9 % respectively, indicating that a significant proportion of silicon content was present in the form of silanol within SYSU-5. Solid state 19F MAS NMR of SYSU-5 exhibited an intense band at −9.3 ppm along with a shoulder band at −20 ppm (Fig. S13), corresponding to the F− ions located in the d4r CBUs consisting of 3 or 4 germanium atoms and d4r with one germanium atom, respectively. The signal at −124.4 ppm was attributed to the free F− ions located in the layers or channels serving as the counterparts for the DESO molecules to balance charge [46]. The structural analysis and related attempts at structural modification are still in progress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The function of TMSF during the crystallization of DESO-SYSU-3
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the role of TMSF cations throughout the whole synthesis process, a series of comparative synthetic experiments were carried out. The synthesis conditions are listed in Table S4. When DESO was used as the sole OSDA under a similar gel composition, only SYSU-5 with low crystallinity was obtained (Fig. S14), proving that DESO molecules could only direct the formation of SYSU-5 under analogous synthetic conditions. The decrease in crystallinity may be due to the lack of germanium, which is the vital raw material to form d4r CBUs. To keep the basicity of the synthetic gel, an equal amount of NaOH was used to substitute TMSF in another comparative experiment, but only a dense phase was gained (Fig. S15). This result could probably caused by the excessive amount of Na+ in the gel, which tended to form small-pore structures or dense phases. Hence, we speculate that TMSF might not only serve as PFA in the formation of DESO-SYSU-3.

It's well known that some amines could induce the formation of specific CBUs, which favour the selective synthesis of the target zeolite. For example, the tetramethylammonium cation (TMA+) could effectively facilitate the generation of sodalite (sod) cage, leading to the successful synthesis of LTA, FAU, SOD, OFF-type zeolites with sod units in structure [47,48]. Taking into account the effective diameter of TMA+ (6.4 Å) and the pore opening of the sod cage (2.8 Å for the 6-ring), TMA+ is supposed to fit into the sod cage rather than diffuse. In this work, the configuration and effective diameter (6.8 Å) of the TMSF cation are similar to the TMA+ to a certain extent (Fig. 5 a). Hence, we suppose that the TMSF cation could probably induce the formation of certain CBUs, playing a similar structure-directing role as TMA+ during the reaction. The -SYT topology consists of two main CBUs d4r and clo cages, where the d4r cage could be stabilized by the fluorine ion, while the clo cage was supposed to be induced as well as stabilized by TMSF cation in the aging stage. During the high temperature crystallization process, the heat-sensitive TMSF cation was gradually decomposed to sulfur dioxide or other small organic species and removed by the washing step.

From the perspective of CDs formation, the increase of alkalinity in hydrothermal systems could significantly accelerate the condensation and carbonization process of precursors [23,49]. In this system, although the sulfur in TMSF cation failed to cooperate into the CDs, the addition of TMSF effectively increased the alkalinity of synthetic gel, thus facilitating the generation of CDs. Meanwhile, the extra-large pore zeolite matrix could restrict the non-radiative vibrations of surface functional groups on CDs as well as protect CDs from environmental water or oxygen quenching [50], which could stabilize the triplet state of the excitons and endow DESO-SYSU-3 with afterglow property. The hypothetical function of TMSF is shown in Fig. 5b.

In summary, the mixed-template method using TMSF and DESO was cost-efficient in the preparation of -SYT type extra-large pore zeolite with enhanced Si/Ge ratio. The unknown germanosilicate SYSU-5 was obtained by using DESO alone. In the mixed-template system, the alkaloid-derived DESO served to direct the formation of extra-large pore structure, while the TMSF had a limited structure-directing effect and mainly acted as a pore-filling agent. Furthermore, the TMSF facilitated the generation of carbon dots by increasing the alkalinity of the gel, endowing DESO-SYSU-3 with impressive fluorescence and afterglow properties. The structure of CDs as well as the photoluminescence mechanism of the CDs@zeolite composite were investigated. This work provides a new perspective for reducing the synthetic cost of extra-large pore zeolites and exploring new CDs@zeolite composite based on extra-large pore zeolitic matrix by adopting a mixed-template strategy.

Jiali Wen: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Chuanqi Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Yang Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Zhifeng Zeng: Investigation, Data curation. Fei Ma: Writing – review & editing. Jiuxing Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21805316 and 22372200).
